html:
XXIX

Miriam had no choice but to settle herself on the cane-seated chair. When Miss Dear had drawn the four drab coloured curtains into place the small cubicle was in semidarkness.

‚ÄúI hope the next time you come to tea with me it will be under rather more comfortable circumstances.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThis is all right,‚ÄĚ said Miriam in abstracted impatient continuation of her abounding manner. Miss Dear was arranging herself on the bed as if for a long sitting. The small matter of business would come now. Having had tea it would be impossible to depart the moment the discussion was over. How much did the tea cost here? That basement tearoom, those excited young women and middle-aged women watchful and stealthy and ugly with poverty and shifts, those teapots and shabby trays and thick bread and butter were like the Y.W.C.A. public restaurant at the other end of the street‚Ā†‚ÄĒfourpence at the outside; but Miss Dear would have to pay it. She felt trapped‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ ‚Äúa few moments of your time to advise me‚ÄĚ and now half the summer twilight had gone and she was pinned in this prison face to face with anything Miss Dear might choose to present; forced by the presences audible in the other cubicles to a continuation of her triumphant tearoom manner.

‚ÄúYou must excuse my dolly.‚ÄĚ She arranged her skirt neatly about the ankle of the slippered bandaged foot.

Anyone else would say what is the matter with your foot.‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ It stuck out, a dreadfully padded mass, dark in the darkness of the dreadful little enclosure in the dreadful dark hive of women, collected together only by poverty.

‚ÄúHave you left your association?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh no, de‚ÄĎer; not permanently of course,‚ÄĚ said Miss Dear pausing in her tweakings and adjustments of draperies to glance watchfully through the gloom.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm still a member there.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh yes.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBut I‚Äôve got to look after myself. They don‚Äôt give you a chance.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNo‚Ā†‚ÄĒ‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs rush in and rush out and rush in and rush out.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat are you going to do?‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ what do you want with me.‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat do you mean de‚ÄĎer.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell I mean, are you going on nursing.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOf course de‚ÄĎer. I was going to tell you.‚ÄĚ

Miriam‚Äôs restive anger would not allow her to attend fully to the long story. She wandered off with the dreadful idea of nursing a ‚Äúsemi-mental‚ÄĚ sitting in a deck-chair in a country garden, the hopeless patient, the nurse half intent on a healthy life and fees for herself, and recalled the sprinkling of uniformed figures amongst the women crowded at the table, all in this dilemma, all eagerly intent; all overworked by associations claiming part of their fees or taking the risks of private nursing, all getting older; all anyhow as long as they went on nursing bound to live on illness; to live with illness knowing that they were living on it. Yet Mr.¬†Leyton had said that no hospital run by a religious sisterhood was any good‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ these women were run by doctors.‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶

‚ÄúYou see de‚ÄĎer it‚Äôs the best thing any sensible nurse can do as soon as she knows a sufficient number of influenchoo peopoo‚Ā†‚ÄĒphysicians and others.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYes, I see.‚ÄĚ‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ But what has all this to do with me.‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶

‚ÄúI shall keep in correspondence with my doctors and friends and look after myself a bit.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYes, I see,‚ÄĚ said Miriam eagerly. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a splendid plan. What did you want to consult me about?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell you see it‚Äôs like this. I must tell you my little difficulty. The folks at thirty-three don‚Äôt know I‚Äôm here and I don‚Äôt want to go back there just at present. I was wondering if when I leave here you‚Äôd mind my having my box sent to your lodgings. I shan‚Äôt want my reserve things down there.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell‚Ā†‚ÄĒthere isn‚Äôt much room in my room.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a flat box. I got it to go to the Colonies with a patient.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, did you go?‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ Nurses did see life; though they were never free to see it in their own way. Perhaps some of them‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ but then they would not be good nurses.

‚ÄúWell I didn‚Äôt go. It was a chance of a lifetime. Such a de‚ÄĎer old gentleman‚Ā†‚ÄĒone of the Fitz-Duff family. It would have been nurse companion. He didn‚Äôt want me in uniform. My word. He gave me a complete outfit, took me round, coats and skirts at Peters, gloves at Penberthy‚Äôs, a lovely gold-mounted umbrella, everything the heart could desire. He treated me just like a daughter.‚ÄĚ During the whole of this speech she redeemed her words by little delicate bridling movements and adjustments, her averted eyes resting in indulgent approval on the old gentleman.

‚ÄúWhy didn‚Äôt you go?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúHe died, dear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh I see.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt could go under your bed, out of the way.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI‚Äôve got hatboxes and things. My room is full of things I‚Äôm afraid.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúP‚Äôraps your landlady would let it stand somewhere.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI might ask her‚Ā†‚ÄĒwon‚Äôt they let you leave things here?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThey would I daresay,‚ÄĚ frowned Miss Dear, ‚Äúbut I have special reasons. I don‚Äôt wish to be beholden to the people here.‚ÄĚ She patted the tendrils of her hair, looking about the cubicle with cold disapproval.

‚ÄúI daresay Mrs.¬†Bailey wouldn‚Äôt mind. But I hardly like to ask her, you know. There seems to be luggage piled up everywhere.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOf course I should be prepared to pay a fee.‚ÄĚ

‚Ä¶ What a wonderful way of living‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ dropping a trunk full of things and going off with a portmanteau; starting life afresh in a new strange place. Miriam regarded the limber capable form outstretched on the narrow bed. This dark little enclosure, the forced companionship of the crowd of competing adventuresses, the sounds of them in the near cubicles, the perpetual sound filling the house like a sea of their busy calculations‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ all this was only a single passing incident‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ beyond it were the wide well-placed lives of wealthy patients.

‚ÄúMiss Younger is a sweet woman.‚ÄĚ

Miriam‚Äôs eyes awoke to affronted surprise.

‚ÄúYou know de‚ÄĎer; the wan yow was sitting by at teatime. I told you just now.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh,‚ÄĚ said Miriam guiltily.

Miss Dear dropped her voice; ‚Äúshe‚Äôs told me her whole story. She‚Äôs a dear sweet Christian woman. She‚Äôs working in a settlement. She‚Äôs privately engaged to the Bishop. It‚Äôs not to be published yet. She‚Äôs a sweet woman.‚ÄĚ

Miriam rose. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve got to get back, I‚Äôm afraid.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt hurry away, dear. I hoped you would stay and have some supper.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI really can‚Äôt,‚ÄĚ said Miriam wearily.

‚ÄúWell, perhaps we shall meet again before Thursday. You‚Äôll ask Mrs.¬†Bailey about my box,‚ÄĚ said Miss Dear getting to her feet.

‚ÄúFancy your remembering her name,‚ÄĚ said Miriam with loud cheerfulness, fumbling with the curtains.

Miss Dear stood beaming indulgently.

All the way down the unlit stone staircase they rallied each other about the country garden with the deck chairs.

‚ÄúWell,‚ÄĚ said Miriam from the street, ‚ÄúI‚Äôll let you know about Mrs.¬†Bailey.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAll right dear, I shall expect to hear from you; au revoir,‚ÄĚ cried Miss Dear from the door. In the joy of her escape into the twilight Miriam waved her hand towards the indulgently smiling form and flung away, singing.
